claims.flip_evidence_text: if (i)(B) an Intervening Event has not occurred and is not a basis for a Board of Directors to submit this Agreement to its stockholders without recommendation, or (ii) such Board of Directors determines that failure to take such actions would not more likely than not result in a violation of its fiduciary duty under applicable law
claims.notes: The claim correctly identifies two necessary substantive conditions but omits the mandatory procedural requirements—compliance with Section 6.12, four business days’ prior written notice, good-faith negotiation, and a post-negotiation Board determination—that the contract explicitly ties with the phrase “if, but only if.”

prompt:
You are a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list.

For each claim return:
- correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes or no
- flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string
- notes: optional short note

Contract excerpt:
6.3 Stockholders' Approvals. <omitted> (b) Notwithstanding anything in this Agreement to the contrary, subject to Section 8.1 and Section 8.2, prior to the receipt of the Requisite BancShares Vote, in the case of BancShares, or the Requisite CIT Vote, in the case of CIT, each of the Boards of Directors of BancShares and CIT may submit this Agreement to its stockholders without recommendation (which, for the avoidance of doubt, shall constitute a Recommendation Change), in which event such Board of Directors may communicate the basis for its lack of recommendation to its stockholders in the Joint Proxy Statement or an appropriate amendment or supplement thereto to the extent required by law (although the resolutions approving this Agreement as of the date hereof may not be rescinded or amended), if (i)(A) such Board of Directors has received an Acquisition Proposal, which it believes in good faith, after receiving the advice of its outside counsel and, with respect to financial matters, its financial advisors, constitutes a Superior Proposal (in which event, subject to compliance with this Section 6.3(b), the Board of Directors of CIT may cause CIT to terminate this Agreement pursuant to Section 8.1(f) and the Board of Directors of BancShares may cause BancShares to terminate this Agreement pursuant to Section 8.1(g)) or (B) an Intervening Event has occurred, and (ii) such Board of Directors, after receiving the advice of its outside counsel and, with respect to financial matters, its financial advisors, determines in good faith that failure to take such actions would more likely than not result in a violation of its fiduciary duty under applicable law, in each case, if, but only if, (1) BancShares and CIT, as applicable, have complied in all material respects with Section 6.12, (2) BancShares or CIT, as applicable, delivers to the other party at least four (4) business days’ prior written notice of its intention to take such action, and furnishes to the other party a reasonable description of the events or circumstances giving rise to its determination to take such action (including, in the event such action is taken in response to an Acquisition Proposal, the identity of the person making such Acquisition Proposal, a copy of the proposed transaction agreement(s) and all other documents relating to such Acquisition Proposal), (3) prior to taking such action, BancShares or CIT, as applicable, negotiates, and causes its financial, legal, and other advisors to negotiate, in good faith with the other party, during the four (4) business day period following BancShares’ or CIT’s, as applicable, delivery of the notice referred to in such sub-clause (2) above (to the extent the party receiving such notice desires to so negotiate), and (4) after the conclusion of such four (4) business day period, the Board of Directors of BancShares or CIT, as applicable, determines in good faith, after giving effect to all of the adjustments (if any) which may be offered by the other party pursuant to sub-clause (3) above, that, in the case of actions described in clause (i) (A) above, such Acquisition Proposal continues to constitute a Superior Proposal and in case of actions described in either clause (i)(A) or clause (i)(B) above, it would nevertheless more likely than not result in a violation of its fiduciary duties under applicable law to make or continue to make the BancShares Board Recommendation or the CIT Board Recommendation, as applicable (it being agreed that, if such actions are being taken in response to an Acquisition Proposal, in the event that, following delivery of the notice referred to in sub-clause (2) above, there is any material revision to the terms of such Acquisition Proposal, including, any revision in price, the four (4) business day period during which the parties agree to negotiate in good faith shall be extended, if applicable, to ensure that at least two (2) business days remain to negotiate subsequent to the time BancShares or CIT, as applicable, notifies the other party of any such material revision (it being understood that there may be multiple extensions)). (Page 58)

Question:
COR permitted in response to Intervening Event

Llama answer:
A corporation may submit this Agreement to its stockholders without recommendation if an Intervening Event has occurred and the Board of Directors determines in good faith that failure to take such actions would more likely than not result in a violation of its fiduciary duty under applicable law.

Canonical claims:
- claim-219376679d76: A corporation may submit this Agreement to its stockholders without recommendation if an Intervening Event has occurred and the Board of Directors determines in good faith that failure to take such actions would more likely than not result in a violation of its fiduciary duty under applicable law.

messages.content:
You are a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list.

For each claim return:
- correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes or no
- flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string
- notes: optional short note

Contract excerpt:
6.3 Stockholders' Approvals. <omitted> (b) Notwithstanding anything in this Agreement to the contrary, subject to Section 8.1 and Section 8.2, prior to the receipt of the Requisite BancShares Vote, in the case of BancShares, or the Requisite CIT Vote, in the case of CIT, each of the Boards of Directors of BancShares and CIT may submit this Agreement to its stockholders without recommendation (which, for the avoidance of doubt, shall constitute a Recommendation Change), in which event such Board of Directors may communicate the basis for its lack of recommendation to its stockholders in the Joint Proxy Statement or an appropriate amendment or supplement thereto to the extent required by law (although the resolutions approving this Agreement as of the date hereof may not be rescinded or amended), if (i)(A) such Board of Directors has received an Acquisition Proposal, which it believes in good faith, after receiving the advice of its outside counsel and, with respect to financial matters, its financial advisors, constitutes a Superior Proposal (in which event, subject to compliance with this Section 6.3(b), the Board of Directors of CIT may cause CIT to terminate this Agreement pursuant to Section 8.1(f) and the Board of Directors of BancShares may cause BancShares to terminate this Agreement pursuant to Section 8.1(g)) or (B) an Intervening Event has occurred, and (ii) such Board of Directors, after receiving the advice of its outside counsel and, with respect to financial matters, its financial advisors, determines in good faith that failure to take such actions would more likely than not result in a violation of its fiduciary duty under applicable law, in each case, if, but only if, (1) BancShares and CIT, as applicable, have complied in all material respects with Section 6.12, (2) BancShares or CIT, as applicable, delivers to the other party at least four (4) business days’ prior written notice of its intention to take such action, and furnishes to the other party a reasonable description of the events or circumstances giving rise to its determination to take such action (including, in the event such action is taken in response to an Acquisition Proposal, the identity of the person making such Acquisition Proposal, a copy of the proposed transaction agreement(s) and all other documents relating to such Acquisition Proposal), (3) prior to taking such action, BancShares or CIT, as applicable, negotiates, and causes its financial, legal, and other advisors to negotiate, in good faith with the other party, during the four (4) business day period following BancShares’ or CIT’s, as applicable, delivery of the notice referred to in such sub-clause (2) above (to the extent the party receiving such notice desires to so negotiate), and (4) after the conclusion of such four (4) business day period, the Board of Directors of BancShares or CIT, as applicable, determines in good faith, after giving effect to all of the adjustments (if any) which may be offered by the other party pursuant to sub-clause (3) above, that, in the case of actions described in clause (i) (A) above, such Acquisition Proposal continues to constitute a Superior Proposal and in case of actions described in either clause (i)(A) or clause (i)(B) above, it would nevertheless more likely than not result in a violation of its fiduciary duties under applicable law to make or continue to make the BancShares Board Recommendation or the CIT Board Recommendation, as applicable (it being agreed that, if such actions are being taken in response to an Acquisition Proposal, in the event that, following delivery of the notice referred to in sub-clause (2) above, there is any material revision to the terms of such Acquisition Proposal, including, any revision in price, the four (4) business day period during which the parties agree to negotiate in good faith shall be extended, if applicable, to ensure that at least two (2) business days remain to negotiate subsequent to the time BancShares or CIT, as applicable, notifies the other party of any such material revision (it being understood that there may be multiple extensions)). (Page 58)

Question:
COR permitted in response to Intervening Event

Llama answer:
A corporation may submit this Agreement to its stockholders without recommendation if an Intervening Event has occurred and the Board of Directors determines in good faith that failure to take such actions would more likely than not result in a violation of its fiduciary duty under applicable law.

Canonical claims:
- claim-219376679d76: A corporation may submit this Agreement to its stockholders without recommendation if an Intervening Event has occurred and the Board of Directors determines in good faith that failure to take such actions would more likely than not result in a violation of its fiduciary duty under applicable law.

message.reasoning:
The user wants me to act as a proxy legal annotator. I need to evaluate the canonical claim against the contract excerpt, question, and model answer (Llama answer).

 Let me break down the task:
 1. Contract excerpt: Section 6.3(b) discusses when Boards can submit the Agreement to stockholders without recommendation (which constitutes a Recommendation Change).
 2. Question: "COR permitted in response to Intervening Event" (COR likely means Change of Recommendation)
 3. Llama answer: States that a corporation may submit this Agreement to stockholders without recommendation if an Intervening Event has occurred and the Board determines in good faith that failure to take such actions would more likely than not result in a violation of fiduciary duty.
 4. Canonical claim: Exactly matches the Llama answer.

 Now I need to verify the claim against the contract text:
 - The contract says: "each of the Boards of Directors of BancShares and CIT may submit this Agreement to its stockholders without recommendation ... if (i) ... (B) an Intervening Event has occurred, and (ii) such Board of Directors ... determines in good faith that failure to take such actions would more likely than not result in a violation of its fiduciary duty under applicable law"
 - Wait, there are additional conditions: (1) compliance with Section 6.12, (2) 4 business days' notice, (3) negotiation during the 4 business day period, (4) after the period, the Board determines that it would nevertheless more likely than not result in a violation of fiduciary duties to continue the recommendation.
 - The claim mentions only two conditions: (1) Intervening Event occurred, and (2) Board determines fiduciary duty violation. It omits the other requirements: compliance with Section 6.12, 4 business days' notice, good faith negotiation, and the subsequent determination after the 4-day period.
 - However, looking at the claim carefully: "A corporation may submit this Agreement to its stockholders without recommendation if an Intervening Event has occurred and the Board of Directors determines in good faith that failure to take such actions would more likely than not result in a violation of its fiduciary duty under applicable law."
 - Is this true? The contract says this is permitted only if the conditions in sub-clauses (1)-(4) are also met. The claim doesn't say "only if" - it says "if". In logic, "if" can mean sufficient conditions, but in legal contexts, stating "X may do Y if Z" when the contract requires Z plus A, B, C, D is misleading because it implies Z is sufficient.
 - However, looking at the model answer (Llama answer), it provides the same text. The question is "COR permitted in response to Intervening Event". The Llama answer captures part of the conditions but omits the procedural safeguards.
 - Is the claim true? The claim is technically partially true because an Intervening Event + fiduciary duty determination are necessary conditions, but not sufficient by themselves. The claim omits material conditions. Therefore, it's not fully true because it suggests these are the only conditions.
 - Wait, let me reconsider. The claim says "A corporation may submit this Agreement to its stockholders without recommendation if an Intervening Event has occurred and..." Does this accurately reflect the contract? The contract excerpt does list those as conditions precedent (i)(B) and (ii), but then adds "in each case, if, but only if" conditions (1)-(4). So the Intervening Event + fiduciary determination are NOT enough. The claim is therefore false or partially true.
 - But let's look at what the claim is actually saying. It's not saying "solely if" or "if and only if". It's saying "may ... if". In natural language, "may do X if Y" often implies Y is one of the conditions, but doesn't necessarily deny other conditions. However, in legal annotation tasks like this, usually claims that omit material conditions are considered "partially_true" or "false" depending on severity.
 - Given that this is a proxy legal annotator task and looking at the contract text, the omitted conditions are substantial (4 business days notice, negotiation, compliance with 6.12, and post-negotiation determination). The claim makes it sound like the only requirements are the Intervening Event and fiduciary duty determination.
 - But wait, is there any nuance? The claim text is identical to the Llama answer. The question asks whether COR is permitted in response to Intervening Event. The answer is yes, subject to conditions. The claim states conditions but not all of them.
 - In these types of annotation tasks, if a claim captures part of the truth but omits material qualifications, it's usually "partially_true". If it's completely wrong, it's "false". Here, the core substance (Intervening Event + fiduciary duty) is correct as far as it goes, but incomplete.
 - Let me check: is the claim "load bearing"? Load bearing means if we flip the evidence text (negate it or change it), it would change the answer. The claim restates part of the contract. The contract does contain this text (or close to it) as conditions. Wait, actually the contract says: "if (i) ... (B) an Intervening Event has occurred, and (ii) such Board of Directors ... determines in good faith that failure to take such actions would more likely than not result in a violation of its fiduciary duty under applicable law, in each case, if, but only if, (1)..."
 - So the exact text about Intervening Event and fiduciary duty is in the contract. The claim quotes essentially that portion. Is it load bearing? The load_bearing_label asks: is this claim essential to the answer? If we flip the evidence (i.e., if the contract did NOT say an Intervening Event allows this), would the answer change? Yes, because the question is whether COR is permitted in response to Intervening Event. The fact that Intervening Event is a basis for this action is load bearing.
 - Wait, "flip_evidence_text" means I need to provide a counterfactual text if load_bearing=yes. The instruction says: "flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string". I need to provide the flipped evidence text that would make the claim false or change the answer.
 - Actually, looking more carefully at the output format, I need to return for the claim:
   - correctness_label
   - load_bearing_label
   - flip_evidence_text (if load_bearing=yes)
   - notes

 Let me think about correctness_label again. The claim says the corporation may submit without recommendation if an Intervening Event has occurred and the Board determines fiduciary duty violation. The contract says they may do so if (i)(B) Intervening Event and (ii) fiduciary duty determination, BUT ONLY IF (1)-(4). So the claim is incomplete. It omits the "but only if" conditions. In legal claim annotation, this is typically "partially_true" because it states necessary conditions but not sufficient ones, or it states part of the requirements accurately.

 However, sometimes if the claim omits critical procedural requirements, it might be considered false because the act is NOT permitted merely upon those two conditions. The phrase "if" in the claim is ambiguous. But given the model answer (Llama answer) says exactly this, and the task is to evaluate the canonical claim, I should evaluate based on the contract.

 Actually, let me reconsider the question. The question is "COR permitted in response to Intervening Event". The Llama answer is essentially a direct quote/summary of the condition. The canonical claim matches the Llama answer. Is the Llama answer correct? It's incomplete. A complete answer would mention the additional conditions. But does the claim accurately reflect the contract? It accurately reflects that these are required conditions, but doesn't mention they are not sufficient alone.

 In many legal NLP datasets (like CUAD or similar), a claim that is incomplete but not incorrect is often labeled "partially_true". But if the omission is material to the permission being granted, it could be false. Here, the permission is explicitly conditioned on (1)-(4). Without those, the Board cannot do it. So saying "A corporation may submit ... if [condition]" when in reality it may do so only if [condition + other conditions] is misleading. However, strictly logically, "if" is not "if and only if". But in common parlance and legal summarization, this is an incomplete statement.

 Let me look at the exact wording of the claim again: "A corporation may submit this Agreement to its stockholders without recommendation if an Intervening Event has occurred and the Board of Directors determines in good faith that failure to take such actions would more likely than not result in a violation of its fiduciary duty under applicable law."

 This is almost verbatim from the contract, except the contract has more conditions. The contract says: "each of the Boards ... may submit this Agreement to its stockholders without recommendation ... if (i) ... (B) an Intervening Event has occurred, and (ii) such Board ... determines in good faith ... in each case, if, but only if, (1) ... (2) ... (3) ... (4) ..."

 So the claim extracts the (i)(B) and (ii) part but drops the "if, but only if" and (1)-(4). This is a significant omission.

 I think "partially_true" is the safest label because the claim is not factually wrong about what the contract says regarding those specific conditions; it's just incomplete regarding the full set of conditions required for the permission.

 Now, load_bearing_label: Is this claim load bearing? Yes, because the question is specifically about whether a Change of Recommendation (COR) is permitted in response to an Intervening Event. The claim establishes that an Intervening Event is indeed a valid basis (one of the bases) for submitting without recommendation. If the contract did not allow this (e.g., if it only allowed Acquisition Proposals), the answer would be different. So the claim is load bearing.

 flip_evidence_text: I need to provide text that flips the evidence. If the evidence text is "an Intervening Event has occurred", the flip would be that an Intervening Event has NOT occurred or is not a basis. Or more precisely, I should provide the counterfactual contract text that would make the claim false. For example: "an Intervening Event has not occurred" or better, quote the contract but change the relevant part to say that an Intervening Event does NOT permit this. Actually, the instructions say "flip_evidence_text". This usually means the negated or contradictory evidence text. A good flip would be: "if ... (B) an Intervening Event has not occurred, or ... the Board of Directors determines ... that failure to take such actions would not result in a violation of its fiduciary duty" or simply the contract text with the Intervening Event clause removed or negated.

 Wait, maybe I should provide the exact contract text but flipped. The contract says: "(B) an Intervening Event has occurred". The flip would be: "(B) an Intervening Event has not occurred" or "(B) no Intervening Event has occurred". But more comprehensively, the flip should be the portion of the contract that contradicts the claim. The claim says Intervening Event is a basis. The flip would be text saying it is NOT a basis.

 Actually, looking at similar tasks, flip_evidence_text usually means: what would the evidence text need to say for the claim to be false? So if the contract said instead: "an Intervening Event shall not be a basis for submitting this Agreement to stockholders without recommendation" that would flip it.

 But I need to make it a string. I'll write something like: "an Intervening Event has not occurred and is not a basis for a Board of Directors to submit this Agreement to its stockholders without recommendation" or more simply negate the key clause.

 Let me reconsider the correctness. Could it be "true"? Some annotators might say it's true because the claim doesn't say "only if" and the conditions mentioned are indeed correct. But the omission of (1)-(4) is huge. The contract explicitly says "if, but only if". That makes the omitted conditions material. So "partially_true" seems right.

 Let me double-check the exact contract wording around "if, but only if": "in each case, if, but only if, (1) BancShares and CIT, as applicable, have complied in all material respects with Section 6.12..." So yes, the contract explicitly frames these as exclusive conditions. Therefore, stating the permission applies "if" only the first two conditions are met is legally inaccurate because it's not sufficient.

 I'll go with:
 - correctness_label: partially_true
 - load_bearing_label: yes
 - flip_evidence_text: [A counterfactual where Intervening Event is not a basis, e.g., "no Intervening Event has occurred" or removing the Intervening Event basis]
 - notes: Omits material procedural conditions (Sections 6.12 compliance, 4-day notice, negotiation, and post-negotiation determination) required by the "if, but only if" clause.

 Actually, let me think once more. Is there a possibility this is "false"? If someone asks "Is COR permitted in response to Intervening Event?" and the claim answers by giving two conditions, implying that's enough, but in reality more is needed, then the claim is false as a standalone statement of what the contract permits. However, the claim doesn't explicitly say "only these conditions". But the natural reading of "A corporation may do X if Y" in response to a permission question does imply Y is the relevant condition. Given the model answer is identical and likely generated by a model, and canonical claims often match model answers, the task might expect us to label model answers that are incomplete as partially_true.

 I'll stick with partially_true.

 For the flip_evidence_text, I'll provide the negated version of the key evidence. Something like: "if (i) ... (B) an Intervening Event has not occurred, and (ii) the Board of Directors determines that failure to take such actions would not result in a violation of its fiduciary duty". Or more simply: "an Intervening Event shall not permit a Board of Directors to submit this Agreement to its stockholders without recommendation." I think a clear negation is best.

 Actually, looking at the contract, the evidence supporting the claim is: "(B) an Intervening Event has occurred, and (ii) such Board of Directors ... determines in good faith that failure to take such actions would more likely than not result in a violation of its fiduciary duty under applicable law". To flip this evidence: "(B) an Intervening Event has not occurred, or such Board of Directors determines that failure to take such actions would not more likely than not result in a violation of its fiduciary duty". But since it's load bearing on the permission, the flip could be: "if ... (B) an Intervening Event has not occurred" or removing the Intervening Event exception entirely.

 I'll provide: "if ... (B) an Intervening Event has not occurred and is not a basis for submitting this Agreement without recommendation, and the Board of Directors determines that failure to take such actions would not more likely than not result in a violation of its fiduciary duty"

(B) an Intervening Event has not occurred, or (ii) such Board of Directors determines that failure to take such actions would not more likely than not result in a violation of its fiduciary duty under applicable law"

E.g., "Notwithstanding anything in this Agreement to the contrary, ... an Intervening Event shall not be a basis for a Board of Directors to submit this Agreement to its stockholders without recommendation."

 I'll write: "an Intervening Event has not occurred and shall not be a basis for a Board of Directors to submit this Agreement to its stockholders without recommendation"

 Hmm, but to be more precise, maybe I should just negate the relevant clause. Let me check if there's a standard format. The prompt doesn't specify the exact format of flip_evidence_text, just that it's required if load_bearing=yes.

 Let me finalize:

 notes: Claim accurately identifies two necessary conditions but omits material additional requirements (compliance with Section 6.12, 4 business days' notice, good-faith negotiation, and post-negotiation Board determination) that are explicitly tied by the phrase "if, but only if."

 Wait, I need to be careful. The claim says "A corporation may submit..." The contract applies to both BancShares and CIT. "A corporation" is a fair generalization.

 Okay, I'll go with that.

 One more check: Is it possible the claim is considered true because the question is simply whether it's permitted, and the claim correctly states the substantive conditions, while the other conditions are procedural? In many reading comprehension contexts, mentioning the substantive trigger (Intervening Event + fiduciary duty) might be considered a correct answer even if procedural details are omitted, unless the question specifically asks about the procedure. But legally, the procedural conditions are mandatory. However, for an NLP pilot, they might accept partially_true. I think partially_true is the most defensible.

The question is "COR permitted in response to Intervening Event". If the claim were false (i.e., Intervening Event is NOT a basis), then the answer would be "no" or different. I'll use: "an Intervening Event shall not permit a Board of Directors to submit this Agreement to its stockholders without recommendation"

 I think that's fine.

 Wait, I just realized: the claim is almost verbatim the Llama answer. In some annotation schemes, if the model answer is incomplete, the claim derived from it is labeled based on whether it's supported. The canonical claim IS the Llama answer. The task says "For each claim return...". There's only one claim here.

 Let me also consider if the answer could be "true" with load_bearing "yes". If the annotator instructions say that a claim is true if it is supported by the text, even if incomplete, then true. But usually, "partially_true" is used when there are omissions that materially affect the meaning. The phrase "if, but only if" makes the omission material. So partially_true.

 Alright, final output format should be something like:

   correctness_label: partially_true

 Let me structure the response as a list/dict for the claim.
